ontology.foodex2_description: Papadum / Papad — dried and roasted legume flatbread
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated roasted-papad node; A0CKP (cereal/legume flatbread, dried) is the closest defensible ancestor. Roasting method noted in classification.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for papad or papadum as a distinct food entity.

allergens: may contain traces of wheat depending on manufacturer

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of roasted papad is 2 pieces (approximately 20 g), as commonly served in Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Papad, roasted — Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, ICMR-NIN
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' (revised edition) for roasted papad (urad dal-based). Per-100 g values used directly. Roasted papad is explicitly listed as a distinct preparation from fried papad in the ICMR-NIN tables.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value for roasted papad was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 48 is estimated from the closest analogous food: black gram (urad dal) preparations, which are consistently reported in the low-GI range (GI 38–55) in Atkinson et al. 2021 and peer-reviewed literature. Roasting without oil does not substantially alter the starch digestibility compared to the raw legume base. GL per serving calculated as: (48 × 10.8 g available carbohydrate per 20 g serving) / 100 = 5.2. Available carbohydrate = carbohydrate_g − dietary_fibre_g = 59.8 − 5.8 = 54.0 g per 100 g; per 20 g serving = 10.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (~1252 mg per 100 g; ~250 mg per 2-piece serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit roasted papad to occasional small portions (1 piece) and account for its sodium contribution (~125 mg per piece) within a daily sodium budget of under 2000 mg, particularly when consumed alongside other salty condiments such as chutneys or pickles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~48) due to high protein and fibre content from black gram base
type2_diabetes.guidance: Roasted papad has a low estimated GI (~48) and a modest glycemic load (~5.2 per 2-piece serving), making it a relatively safe occasional snack for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, though portion control remains important given its moderate carbohydrate density.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium content is a cardiovascular risk factor; however, fat content is low and cholesterol is absent
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Roasted papad is low in fat and cholesterol-free, which is favourable for cardiovascular health, but its high sodium content (~1252 mg per 100 g) poses a risk for blood pressure management and should be consumed sparingly by individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roasted papad (also called roasted papadum or papadom) is a thin, crispy Indian flatbread-style wafer made primarily from black gram flour (urad dal), seasoned with salt, black pepper, cumin, and asafoetida. Unlike fried papad, roasted papad is cooked over an open flame or dry heat, resulting in a lighter, lower-fat product with characteristic charred blisters and an airy, crunchy texture. It is a staple accompaniment in Indian cuisine, served alongside dal, rice, curries, and chutneys, and is widely available in Indian restaurants across Singapore. Nutritionally, roasted papad is notable for its high protein content (~22.5 g per 100 g) derived from the black gram base, its moderate dietary fibre (~5.8 g per 100 g), and its very low fat content (~1.9 g per 100 g), making it one of the lower-calorie crispy snack options. However, it carries a very high sodium load (~1252 mg per 100 g), which is a significant concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Its estimated glycemic index of approximately 48 places it in the low-GI category, making it relatively suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in small portions. A standard restaurant serving in Singapore is 2 pieces (~20 g), contributing approximately 71 kcal, 4.5 g protein, and 250 mg sodium per serving.
common_user_queries: How many calories in roasted papad?
Can I eat papad if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: ICMR-NIN lists roasted papad as a distinct entry, providing a strong nutritional anchor. GI is estimated from analogous black gram preparations in peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2021) as no direct roasted-papad GI value exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Serving size of 20 g (2 pieces) is estimated from standard Indian restaurant practice in Singapore, as no standardised portion weight was available from the field survey source data.
notes: 1. Nutrient values sourced from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' for roasted papad (urad dal-based). 2. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate based on black gram preparations per Atkinson et al. 2021 (AJCN); no direct roasted-papad GI entry exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 3. Serving size of 20 g (2 pieces) is a reasonable estimate for restaurant-served papad; actual weight may vary by restaurant. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this entry. 5. polyunsaturated_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this entry. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as roasted papad contains no added hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero by product nature. 7. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this entry. 8. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this entry. 9. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this entry. 10. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this entry. 11. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this entry. 12. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this entry. 13. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as roasted papad is a plant-based legume product; naturally zero. 14. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 as roasted papad contains no vitamin C-bearing ingredients; naturally zero. 15. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients; naturally zero. 16. FoodEx2 code A0CKP used as closest defensible ancestor; no dedicated roasted-papad node exists in FoodEx2. 17. Input food_group was 'Mixed'; reclassified to 'Snacks' as roasted papad is a crispy snack/accompaniment, not a mixed dish.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft constructed. GL recalculated from 5.7 to 5.2 using correct available carbohydrate formula. gi_source match notes updated to reflect corrected GL.

Iteration 2: Corrected ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'analogous_estimate' to 'closest_parent' to comply with G5 hard gate. Updated glycemic_load_per_serving field to 5.2. Verified aliases_by_language consistency — all entries confirmed present verbatim in flat aliases list. Added 'पापड़' and 'Pappadam' and 'Bhuna hua Papad' to flat aliases. Verified all -1 sentinel values documented in notes. Verified zero-macro justification phrases present in notes for cholesterol, vitamin_c, vitamin_b12. Final self-score: 8/8, all hard gates pass.
